title: Variational Autoencoder
text: References Doersch C. Tutorial on Variational Autoencoders[J]. 2016. Link 0. Generative modelingInstead, one often cares about producing more examples that are like those already in a database, but not exactly the same. We can formalize this setup by saying that we get examples $X$ distributed according to some unknown distribution $ P_{gt}(X) $. our goal is to learn a model $P$ which we can sample from, such that $P$ is as similar as possible to $P_{gt}(X)$. Training this type of model has been a long-standing problem in the machine learning community, and classically, most approaches have had one of three serious drawbacks. Require strong assumptions about the structure in the data. Make severe approximations, leading to suboptimal models. Rely on computationally expensive inference procedures like Markov Chain Monte Carlo. More recently, some works have made tremendous progress in training neural networks as powerful function approximators through backpropagation. These advances have given rise to promising frameworks which can use backpropagation-based function approximators to build generative models. 1.1 Preliminaries: Latent Variable ModelFormally, say we have a vector of latent variables $z$ in a high-dimensional space $Z$ which we can easily sample according to some probability density function (PDF) $P(z)$ defined over $Z$. Then, say we have a family of deterministic functions $f (z; \theta)$, parameterized by a vector $\theta$ in some space $\Theta$, where $f : Z × \Theta \rightarrow X$ . f is deterministic, butif $z$ is random and $q$ is fixed, then $f (z; q) $is a random variable in the space $X$. We wish to optimize $q$ such that we can sample $z$ from $P(z)$ and, with high probability, $f (z; q)$ will be like the $X$’s in our dataset. 2 Variational AutoencodersThe mathematical basis of VAEs actually has relatively little to do with classical autoencoders, e.g. sparse autoencoders or denoising autoencoders. They are called “autoencoders” only because the final training objective that derives from this setup does have an encoder and a decoder, and resembles a traditional autoencoder. To solve Equation 1, there are two problems that VAEs must deal with: how to define the latent variables $z$ (i.e., decide what information they represent) how to deal with the integral over $z$. VAEs give a definite answer to both. They assume that there is no simple interpretation of the dimensions of $z$, and instead assert that samples of z can be drawn from a simple distribution, i.e., $N (0, I)$, where $I$ is the identity matrix. In fact, recall that $P(X|z; θ) = N (X| f(z; θ), σ^2 * I)$. If $f(z; θ)$ is a multi-layer neural network, then we can imagine the network using its first few layers to map the normally distributed $z$’s to the latent values (like digit identity, stroke weight, angle, etc.) with exactly the right statitics. 2.1 Setting up the objectiveThe key idea behind the variational autoencoder is to attempt to sample values of $z$ that are likely to have produced $X$, and compute $P(X)$ just from those. This means that we need a new function $Q(z|X)$ which can take a value of $X$ and give us a distribution over $z$ values that are likely to produce $X$.

title: Variational inference : A Review
text: 参考文献 Variational Inference: A Review for Statisticians [PDF]. Variational Inference tutorial from Youtube [PlayList]. 1. 为什么要引入变分推理？在贝叶斯模型中， 我们通过引入图模型，可以轻易地得到一个联合分布，但是想推出条件分布却是很困难的。 推断问题（Inference Problem）是计算给定观测的隐变量的条件分布 $p(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})$。这个条件分布可被用于产生数据，对隐变量进行区间估计，或者得到新数据的预测分布等等。 在推断问题中，隐变量 $\mathbf{z}$ 和观测变量 $\mathbf{x}$ 的联合分布 $p(\mathbf{z},\mathbf{x})$ 是已知的，但观测变量的边缘分布 $p(\mathbf{x})$ 是未知的。通过条件概率公式，我们可以得到下面的式子， $$ \begin{equation}p(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) = \frac{p(\mathbf{z},\mathbf{x})}{p(\mathbf{x}) } = \frac{p(\mathbf{z},\mathbf{x})}{\int p(\mathbf{z},\mathbf{x}) d\mathbf{z}}\end{equation} $$ 在上述式子中，分母是观测变量 $\mathbf{x}$ 的边缘分布$p(\mathbf{x})$ ，也被称作 证据因子（evidence）。 我们通过积分掉隐变量 $\mathbf{z}$ 来得到边缘分布。 然而在大部分的模型当中，分母 $\int p(\mathbf{z},\mathbf{x})d\mathbf{z}$ 的求解通常没有闭式解，或者需要大量的代价去求解甚至是无法积分。这个问题就导致了一些模型难以推断。 为了解决上述问题，常见的方法有： 采样方法（Metropolis Hosting） 变分推理（Variational Inference） 拉普拉斯近似（Laplace approximate） 2. 变分推理（Variational inference）变分推理的目标是，得到给定观测的隐变量的条件分布 $p(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})$ 的一个近似分布 $q(\mathbf{z}) $。 2.1 变分推理问题假定一组概率密度函数 $\mathscr{Q}$，在这一组密度函数中，找到一个密度函数能与后验概率 $p(\mathbf{z},\mathbf{x})$ 最为相似。然而，这个相似程度要如何评判呢？ 我们使用KL散度（Kullback-Leibler divergence）来度量两个分布之间的相似程度。 于是，我们先做一些数学定义，假定一个联合分布，含有隐变量 $ \mathbf{z}=z_{1:n} $ 与观测变量 $\mathbf{x} = x_{1:n}$, $$ \begin{equation}p(\mathbf{z},\mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{z})p(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z}) \end{equation} $$ 使用最优化方法最小化KL散度（Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence），从而得到真实分布的近似分布， $$ \begin{equation}q^∗(\mathbf{z}) =\mathop{\arg\min}_{q(\mathbf{z}) \in \mathscr{Q} }{\text{KL}[q(\mathbf{z}) || p(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})]}\end{equation} $$ 但是， $\mathscr{Q}$ 又要如何进行选择呢？ $\mathscr{Q}$ 的选择，既要足够灵活足以匹配后验概率分布 $p(\mathbf{z},\mathbf{x})$， 又要足够简单来应付优化问题。但这个问题不属于这篇文章的范畴，我们将在后续的文章中继续讨论这个问题。 2.2 证据下界（The evidence lower bound）从公式（3）中，我们将KL散度提取出来看， $$ \begin{equation}\text{KL}[q(\mathbf{z}) || p(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})] = \text{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim q}[log( q(\mathbf{z}))]−\text{E}_{\mathbf{z} \sim q}[log(p(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}))]\end{equation} $$ 公式（4）中的所有期望对应的概率密度函数都是 $q(\mathbf{z})$ 。 将其展开我们的得到， $$ \begin{equation}\text{KL}[q(\mathbf{z}) || p(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})] = \text{E}[log(q(z))] −\text{E}[log(p(z, x))]+ log(p(x)).\end{equation} $$ 计算 $log(p(\mathbf{x})) - 公式（5）$ ，我们得到， $$ \begin{equation}\text{ELBO}(q) =\text{E}[log(p(\mathbf{z}, \mathbf{x}))] − \text{E}[log(q(\mathbf{z}))].\end{equation} $$ 我们把这个函数称作 证据下界（evidence lower bound, ELBO），我们将ELBO函数与KL散度相加，可以得到， $$ \begin{equation}\text{ELBO}(q) + \text{KL}[q(\mathbf{z}) || p(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})] = log(p(\mathbf{x})).\end{equation} $$ 这里由于 $log(p(\mathbf{x}))$ 中的观测 $\mathbf{x}$ 是给定的，该项 $log(p(\mathbf{x}))$ 为常数 $K$。 ELBO增大的同时，KL散度会随之减小，因此，最大化ELBO函数与最小化KL散度是等价的。 我们对公式（6）再进行一些拆分，可以得到， $$\text{ELBO}(q) = \text{E}[log(p(\mathbf{z}))] + \text{E}[log(p(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z}))] - \text{E}[log(q(\mathbf{z}))]$$ $$ \begin{equation}\text{ELBO}(q) = \text{E}[log(p(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z}))] - \text{KL}[q(\mathbf{z}) || p(\mathbf{z})]\end{equation} $$ 我们再回过头来看公式（7）可以发现，对于任意的 $q(\mathbf{z})$，都有 $log(p(\mathbf{x})) \geq ELBO(q)$。 这也正是证据下界（也称变分下界）的名字由来。优化公式（3）也就意味着，我们希望下界与证据因子能够尽可能地紧密，也就是说希望他们之间相差的KL散度能尽可能地小。 以上就是变分推断的入门内容，在接下来的文章里，我们将针对 $\mathscr{Q}$ 的选择问题进行讨论。 （未完待续）
